content.text: Keeping every trace and log line forever is neither affordable nor useful.
Retention tier policies

define how long each class of observability data stays queryable, when it moves to cheaper storage, and when it is deleted — balancing the operational need to investigate recent problems against the cost of storing data whose value decays with age.
What it is

A retention tier policy assigns each stream of traces and logs to a lifecycle of storage tiers — typically hot, warm, and cold — each with a different cost, query latency, and duration. Hot storage keeps recent data instantly queryable for active debugging; warm storage keeps older data available at higher latency and lower cost; cold storage archives data cheaply for the rare case it is needed. The policy specifies, per data class, how long data lives in each tier before transitioning or being deleted.
This is distinct from compliance retention, which mandates minimum durations for legal reasons. Operational retention tiering is driven by the
decaying value of observability data
: a trace is extremely valuable in the minutes after it is emitted, still useful for a few weeks, and rarely consulted after that. Tiering matches storage spend to that value curve.
Why it matters

Observability data volume grows with traffic and with instrumentation richness, and for agent systems it grows fast — every session can emit dozens of spans and log lines. Stored naively in hot storage, this becomes one of the largest line items in an infrastructure budget. Yet most of that data is queried, if ever, only in the first days after emission. Without a retention policy, teams either overspend keeping cold data hot or panic-delete data they later need. A tiered policy makes the trade-off explicit and automatic, so cost tracks value and nothing is deleted by accident.
Benefits
Storage cost tracks data value: expensive hot storage holds only recent, frequently-queried data.
Automatic tier transitions remove the need for manual data lifecycle management.
Predictable, bounded storage growth because every stream has a defined end-of-life.
Recent-incident debugging stays fast because hot storage is not diluted by old data.
Cold archival preserves rare-but-critical historical data at a fraction of hot-storage cost.
Per-class policies let high-value streams (errors) live longer than low-value ones (debug logs).
Trade-offs and limitations
Querying warm or cold data is slower, so an investigation into an old incident is less responsive.
Cold-storage retrieval can incur both latency (hours) and per-retrieval cost.
Choosing tier durations wrong either overspends (too-long hot) or loses needed data (too-short retention).
Downsampling older data to save space discards detail that a deep historical investigation might want.
Multiple tiers and per-class rules add configuration complexity and more ways to misconfigure.
How it works

Each stream is tagged with a data class (for example error-traces, request-logs, debug-logs) that determines its retention schedule. A lifecycle engine — object-storage lifecycle rules, or a time-series database's built-in retention — moves data between tiers based on age. A common schedule keeps data hot for a few days, warm for a few weeks, and cold for months, then deletes it. Older data is often downsampled or compacted before archival: traces may be reduced to a sampled subset, and logs may be aggregated into summary metrics, preserving trend information at a fraction of the size.

The right hot-tier duration follows from how query frequency decays with data age. If query rate falls off roughly exponentially with age, the hot window should cover the age range that captures the large majority of queries. A simple cost model makes the trade-off concrete:
where
is the data volume in tier
its unit storage cost, and
the duration data spends there. Because
, moving the bulk of aged data to cold storage dominates the savings.
Architecture

attrs.alt: Data lifecycle moving from hot to warm to cold storage and finally deletion

content.text: Data is emitted into the hot tier for immediate querying. A lifecycle engine transitions it to warm storage after the hot window, optionally downsampling, then to cold archival, and finally deletes it at end-of-life. Each transition is driven by the data's age and class, and the whole lifecycle runs automatically without manual intervention.
When to use it
Any observability deployment where storage cost is material and data volume grows with traffic.
Systems with a clear decay in query value with data age (most agent request telemetry).
When different data classes have genuinely different value lifetimes (errors vs debug logs).
Less necessary at small scale where all data fits cheaply in hot storage for its full useful life.
How to implement
Classify streams by data class and assign each a value lifetime based on how long it is queried.
Define tier durations per class: hot window, warm window, cold window, then delete.
Configure the storage backend's lifecycle rules to transition data between tiers by age.
Downsample or compact data on transition to warm/cold where full fidelity is not needed.
Measure query rate versus data age to validate that the hot window covers most queries.
Model tier costs and iterate the durations to hit a target spend without losing needed access.
Alert on unexpected volume growth in the hot tier, which signals a policy or instrumentation change.
Examples

# Cost model to choose tier durations
TIERS = {  # unit cost per GB-month
    "hot":  0.023,
    "warm": 0.0125,
    "cold": 0.004,
}

def monthly_cost(gb_per_day, hot_days, warm_days, cold_days):
    def gb_in(days):
        return gb_per_day * days
    hot = gb_in(hot_days) * TIERS["hot"]
    warm = gb_in(warm_days) * TIERS["warm"]
    cold = gb_in(cold_days) * TIERS["cold"]
    return round((hot + warm + cold) / 30 * 30, 2), {
        "hot": round(hot, 2), "warm": round(warm, 2), "cold": round(cold, 2)}

# Compare all-hot vs tiered for 50 GB/day over ~6 months
all_hot = 50 * 180 * TIERS["hot"]
tiered, breakdown = monthly_cost(50, 7, 23, 150)
print("all-hot:", round(all_hot, 2), "tiered:", tiered, breakdown)
Real-world example

An agent platform emitting 80 GB of traces and logs per day kept everything in hot storage for 90 days, costing roughly $16,000 a month and rising. A query-age analysis showed 92% of all trace queries touched data less than five days old, and virtually none touched data older than 30 days except for occasional audit lookups. They moved to a tiered policy: hot for 5 days, warm for 30, cold for 12 months, with error-traces retained longer than debug logs, and tail-sampled traces downsampled before cold archival.

Hot-tier volume dropped by over 90%, cutting the fast-storage bill dramatically while preserving instant access to the recent data engineers actually queried. The rare historical lookups now retrieve from cold storage in a few hours — acceptable for audit use cases — and total observability storage spend fell by roughly 70% with no measurable impact on day-to-day debugging.
Common mistakes
Applying one retention duration to all data classes, overspending on low-value logs or under-retaining errors.
Setting the hot window from intuition rather than measured query-versus-age data.
Forgetting per-retrieval cold-storage costs, so a large historical query becomes surprisingly expensive.
Downsampling so aggressively that archived data can no longer answer the questions it was kept for.
No alert on hot-tier volume, so an instrumentation change silently balloons cost before anyone notices.
Best practices
Drive the hot-tier duration from measured query-frequency-versus-age, not a round number.
Give each data class its own schedule; errors and audit trails outlive debug logs.
Downsample or compact on transition to cheaper tiers, keeping trends without full fidelity.
Model tier costs before committing, and revisit as volume and query patterns change.
Alert on hot-tier volume growth to catch instrumentation or policy regressions early.
Performance and scalability

Tiering directly improves hot-tier query performance because the hot index stays small — queries scan only recent data. As traffic grows, the hot tier grows only with the recent-data window, not with total history, so query latency stays roughly constant while cold archival absorbs the accumulating volume. The scaling risk is on retrieval from cold storage: a broad historical query can trigger many restore operations, each with latency and cost. Mitigate by indexing cold data by time and class so historical queries restore only the relevant objects, and by keeping a lightweight, always-hot summary (downsampled metrics) that answers most historical trend questions without a cold restore at all.

Security and reliability

Retention policies are a reliability control as much as a cost control: an accidental over-aggressive expiration rule can delete data you needed for an active investigation. Guard expiration rules with review and stage them (transition first, delete much later) so there is a window to catch mistakes. Ensure lifecycle transitions preserve encryption — data moving to cold storage must remain encrypted with a managed key. For streams that also carry compliance obligations, the operational tier policy must never delete data before its compliance minimum; layer the two policies so the longer of the operational and compliance durations always wins, and test that the deletion job honours compliance holds.
Mental model

Think of retention tiers like the way an office handles paper. Today's active files sit on the desk (hot) where they are grabbed constantly. Last month's move to the filing cabinet (warm) — still reachable, a few seconds slower. Last year's go to an offsite storage box (cold) that takes a day to recall and is almost never opened. Eventually the retention schedule shreds them. Nobody keeps ten years of paper on their desk; the cost in space would be absurd, and the value of a decade-old memo on the desk is near zero. Observability data has the same value-versus-age curve — tiering is just applying the filing discipline automatically.
Downsampling traces before archival

Full-fidelity traces are what make hot storage expensive, and most of that fidelity is worthless once an incident window has passed. Downsampling on transition to cheaper tiers keeps the information that retains value — error traces, a representative sample of successful traces, and aggregate latency distributions — while discarding the bulk of routine spans. A common approach retains 100% of error and slow traces, a small percentage of normal traces, and rolls the rest into summary metrics that preserve trend information at a tiny fraction of the size.

The key discipline is to decide what historical questions the archive must answer, and downsample to preserve exactly those. If the archive exists to investigate rare error patterns and to show long-term latency trends, then keeping all errors plus downsampled latency histograms answers both without storing every successful span.

attrs.alt: Traces downsampled progressively as they move from hot to warm to cold storage

content.text: Decide which historical questions the archive must answer, then downsample to preserve only those.
Retain 100% of error and slow traces; they are rare and carry the most diagnostic value.
Keep a small representative sample of successful traces for context.
Roll the remaining routine traces into latency histograms that preserve trends cheaply.
Strip verbose attributes from sampled traces before archival to shrink them further.
Validate that a downsampled archive can still answer the questions it was designed for.
Key takeaways
Retention tiers match storage cost to the decaying value of observability data with age.
Set the hot-tier window from measured query-versus-age data, not a guessed round number.
Give each data class its own schedule so errors outlive low-value debug logs.
Downsample or compact on transition to cheaper tiers to keep trends without full fidelity.
Stage deletions and layer with compliance holds so nothing needed is ever deleted early.
Keep an always-hot downsampled summary to answer historical trends without a cold restore.
